_purpose: Seasonal calendar for the IL Central Sangamon cluster. Month-by-month agricultural, social, economic, and weather patterns across two eras (1818-1880 frontier/antebellum agriculture, 1880-1960 mechanized Corn Belt agriculture). The central Illinois calendar was governed by corn — planting in May, cultivation in June-July, harvest in October-November — with the rhythms of government in Springfield and industry in Decatur layered on top.
display_name: IL Central Sangamon — Seasonal Calendar Profile
primary_economy: Corn and livestock agriculture (1818-1960); state government (Springfield, 1837-present); agribusiness (Decatur, 1912-present); coal mining (Christian/Shelby, 1880-1940)

calendar_eras.description: The agricultural calendar governed central Illinois life from statehood through the first generation of commercial farming. Corn was the dominant crop, supplemented by wheat, oats, and livestock (hogs and cattle). The growing season ran from late April through October, with the corn harvest determining the year's economic fate. The lay-by period in July-August — when the corn was growing but required no cultivation — was the one break the calendar allowed. Court sessions, elections, and social events were scheduled around the agricultural calendar.

january.agricultural: Fields frozen and dormant. Livestock feeding from stored hay and corn. Fence repair with osage orange posts and split rails. Hog killing if not completed in December. Timber cutting for fuel and building.
january.social_civic: Limited social activity. Church services (Baptist, Methodist, Christian/Disciples). Court sessions in county seats. Legislature convened in Springfield (January-March typically).
january.economic: No farm income. Credit accumulating at the crossroads store. Corn from the fall harvest being hauled to market when roads were frozen solid enough to bear wagon weight. If roads were mud-locked, surplus corn waited in the crib.
january.weather_conditions: Cold — average 22-30F. Hard freezes common. Heavy snow possible. The flat terrain meant wind chill was severe — no hills, no timber to break the wind on the open prairie.
january.narrative_hook: January in {county} County was when the prairie showed its teeth — {ancestor_name} feeding stock in twenty-degree wind with nothing between the barn and the Dakotas but a fence post.

february.agricultural: Late winter fieldwork beginning on warmer days. Breaking ground preparation. Repairing equipment for spring.
february.social_civic: Limited. Lincoln's birthday (February 12) became a holiday after 1865 — Springfield commemorated it formally.
february.economic: Pre-season expenses mounting. Seed purchases. Equipment repair costs.
february.weather_conditions: Late winter — 24-34F. Ice storms the greatest hazard. Prairie roads alternately frozen and mud-locked.
february.narrative_hook: February was when {ancestor_name} counted what was left from the fall corn money and calculated whether the family could reach planting without going deeper to the merchant.

march.agricultural: Plowing began in earnest on warmer days. Prairie soil turned black and gleaming behind the steel plow. Oats planted early. Garden preparation near the house.
march.social_civic: Easter observances. Spring court session. Prairie burning (early settlers sometimes burned the prairie to clear dead grass — the last echo of the Native practice).
march.economic: Seed and supply purchases. Credit extension. The annual cycle of debt renewed.
march.weather_conditions: Variable — 34-48F. Thaw-freeze cycles made roads impassable. 'Mud season' was the defining transportation fact of March.
march.narrative_hook: March in {county} County was mud season — {ancestor_name} watching the black soil thaw and calculating when the ground was dry enough to take a plow without drowning the horses.

april.agricultural: Corn planting preparation. Fields plowed and harrowed. Oats up. Garden planted. The timing of corn planting was critical — too early risked frost damage; too late reduced the growing season.
april.social_civic: Easter services. Circuit court. Community gathered for planting season.
april.economic: Full credit extension. Seed corn purchased. The farmer was committed to the year's crop.
april.weather_conditions: Warming — 48-58F. Thunderstorms. Frost risk until mid-April. Tornado season beginning.
april.narrative_hook: April was when {ancestor_name} watched the sky — waiting for the frost to pass, praying for rain but not too much, and knowing that the timing of the first corn in the ground would determine October's harvest.

may.agricultural: Corn planting — the defining event of the agricultural year. Seeds dropped in furrows by hand or with a hand planter. Cultivation began as soon as the corn emerged. Every available hand in the field.
may.social_civic: Minimal social activity. Planting consumed all available time and labor.
may.economic: No income. Credit accumulating. The lean season.
may.weather_conditions: Warm — 58-68F. Thunderstorms. Late frost possible. Tornado season.
may.narrative_hook: May was corn planting — {ancestor_name} in the field from sunrise to dark, dropping seed in black furrows that ran to the horizon, betting the family's year on the weather and the worm.

june.agricultural: Corn cultivation — hoeing between rows, running the cultivator. Wheat harvest (where grown). Hay cutting first crop.
june.social_civic: Limited. Some churches held Strawberry Festivals.
june.weather_conditions: Hot — 68-78F. Humidity rising. Thunderstorms frequent and violent. Tornado season at peak.
june.narrative_hook: June was cultivation — {ancestor_name} with a hoe between the corn rows, fighting weeds in black soil that grew them as readily as corn.

july.agricultural: Lay-by — the corn was growing on its own. The one respite in the agricultural calendar. Wheat threshing. Hay second cutting. Some families visited kin during lay-by.
july.social_civic: Fourth of July — the primary summer social event. Barbecues, speeches, and political rallies. The Lincoln-Douglas debate tradition made political speech a July entertainment. Revival season began.
july.economic: No farm income. Odd jobs for cash. The lay-by period was the only time a farmer might work for wages.
july.weather_conditions: Hottest month — 72-86F. Drought risk. The flat terrain trapped humidity. Heat exhaustion was common.
july.narrative_hook: The Fourth of July was when {county} County came together — {ancestor_name} at the barbecue and the speechifying, the corn growing itself for a few weeks, and the politicians making promises in the same square where Lincoln had made his.

august.agricultural: Corn tasseling and filling. Critical growth period. Drought at this stage was catastrophic. Late hay cutting.
august.social_civic: Revival season at peak — Baptist and Methodist camp meetings. Courtship season at the revival. County agricultural fairs.
august.economic: Anticipation of harvest. Feed and supply planning.
august.weather_conditions: Hot and humid — 70-84F. Drought or excessive rain could destroy the crop at this stage.
august.narrative_hook: August revival was when {ancestor_name} heard something other than the corn — the preacher's voice, the singing, and a few evenings that belonged to something larger than the harvest.

september.agricultural: Early corn harvest beginning. Hay final cutting. Preparation for the main harvest.
september.social_civic: School opened (rural schools often started late due to harvest labor needs). County fairs.
september.economic: First harvest income anticipated. The year's economic fate approaching.
september.weather_conditions: Moderating — 62-74F. First cool nights. Frost risk by late September.
september.narrative_hook: September was when {ancestor_name} began to know whether the year had won or lost — the corn ears heavy or light, the weather cooperating or not.

october.agricultural: Corn harvest at peak — husking, shocking, hauling to the crib or the elevator. Every hand in the field. Hogs fattening for December slaughter.
october.social_civic: County court session. Harvest dominated all activity.
october.economic: Primary income month. Corn sold at the elevator or stored in the crib for higher winter prices. The crop-lien debt settled against the harvest.
october.weather_conditions: Cool and pleasant — 50-62F. First frost by mid-October. The one comfortable month for outdoor labor.
october.narrative_hook: October was the reckoning — {ancestor_name} at the elevator in {town} watching the corn weighed, the price per bushel posted on the board, and the year's work reduced to a number.

november.agricultural: Late corn harvest. Fall plowing — turning the fields for spring. Livestock consolidated for winter feeding.
november.social_civic: Thanksgiving. Hunting season — deer, turkey, prairie chicken provided protein.
november.economic: Final corn sales. Debt settlement. Planning for next year.
november.weather_conditions: Cold arriving — 36-46F. First hard freeze. Gray skies and bare fields.
november.narrative_hook: By November {year}, {ancestor_name} knew the answer — the corn money counted against the store debt, and whether Christmas brought rest or anxiety depended on the arithmetic.

december.agricultural: Hog killing — the central food event of the year. Meat curing. Fields dormant. Firewood cutting.
december.social_civic: Christmas — the paramount social event. Church services. Family visiting. Community gatherings.
december.economic: Year-end accounting. The crop cycle closed. Families who cleared their debt had a moment of freedom; those who did not entered January already in the hole.
december.weather_conditions: Cold — 24-34F. Snow. Hard freezes. The prairie wind made the cold penetrating.
december.narrative_hook: Hog killing in December was when {ancestor_name} secured the family's protein for the year — the smell of scalded hog, rendered lard, and curing salt was the smell of winter in {county} County.

calendar_eras.description: Mechanization transformed the agricultural calendar without eliminating it. The corn planter replaced hand planting, the cultivator replaced the hoe, and eventually the combine replaced the husking crew — but the rhythm remained: plant in May, cultivate in June, harvest in October. What changed was labor — fewer people could farm more land. The calendar of the state government employee in Springfield and the factory worker in Decatur operated independently of the corn cycle, but the agricultural calendar still governed the rural counties. The coal miners in Christian and Shelby counties worked year-round underground, oblivious to the seasons except when the mine flooded in spring.

january.agricultural: Livestock feeding. Equipment maintenance in heated machine sheds. Corn-hog farmers marketing stored corn or feeding it to hogs for spring market.
january.social_civic: Legislature convened in Springfield. County Farm Bureau annual meeting. Church. Basketball season in high schools.
january.economic: Grain marketing decisions — sell stored corn or hold for higher prices. State government payroll steady in Springfield. Staley plant in Decatur operating year-round.
january.weather_conditions: Cold — 20-28F. Blizzards possible. Wind chill severe on the flat prairie.
january.narrative_hook: January {year} was corn-hog economics — {ancestor_name} calculating whether to sell the stored corn at today's price or feed it to hogs and sell the pork in April, and the answer depended on the ratio between corn and hog prices at the Chicago Board of Trade.

february.agricultural: Equipment repair. Seed corn selection and purchase. Fertilizer planning.
february.social_civic: Lincoln's Birthday (state holiday). Springfield commemorations at the Lincoln Tomb.
february.economic: Pre-season expenses. Farm Bureau cooperative purchasing. Seed corn dealers making rounds.
february.weather_conditions: Late winter — 22-32F. Ice storms. Roads improving with gravel and early paving.
february.narrative_hook: Lincoln's Birthday in Springfield was a state holiday — {ancestor_name} could visit the tomb and reflect that the president who had split rails on this same prairie would not recognize the combine that now harvested what his axe had cleared.

march.agricultural: Soil preparation beginning. Oats planted. Fertilizer applied. Tractor replacing horses — by 1940, most farms had tractors.
march.social_civic: Spring court. Easter preparation. 4-H clubs organizing for the county fair season.
march.economic: Input costs rising — fuel, fertilizer, seed. The mechanized farm required more capital per acre but less labor.
march.weather_conditions: Variable — 32-48F. Mud season diminishing as roads improved but still a factor on unpaved county roads.
march.narrative_hook: March was when {ancestor_name} started the tractor — or in earlier years, harnessed the team — and broke ground for the corn that would determine the year.

april.agricultural: Field preparation at peak. Corn planting preparation. Alfalfa and clover fields green. Dairy operations (where present) resuming pasture.
april.social_civic: Easter. Spring school events. Baseball season opening.
april.economic: Full input expenditure. The financial commitment to the year's crop was made.
april.weather_conditions: Warming — 48-58F. Tornado season. Spring storms violent on the flat prairie.
april.narrative_hook: April was when {ancestor_name} watched the weather and the calendar — the corn had to go in the ground between late April and mid-May, and every day of delay was yield lost.

may.agricultural: Corn planting — mechanized by 1920 with two-row planters, by 1950 with four-row planters. Planting that had taken weeks by hand now took days by machine. Soybean planting following corn.
may.social_civic: Memorial Day — significant in a region that had sent thousands to the Civil War. Cemetery decorations.
may.economic: No harvest income yet. Credit extended.
may.weather_conditions: Warm — 58-70F. Rain critical for germination. Late frost rare but devastating.
may.narrative_hook: The corn planter covered in a day what {ancestor_name}'s grandfather had taken a week to plant by hand — the machine changed the scale, but the gamble on weather and price was the same.

june.agricultural: Corn cultivation with tractor-mounted cultivators. Soybean cultivation. Hay cutting. Wheat harvest (where grown).
june.social_civic: Strawberry festivals. School letting out. Summer social season beginning.
june.narrative_hook: June thunderstorms on the Illinois prairie came without warning — {ancestor_name} watched the western horizon for the green-black clouds that meant hail, and hail meant the corn was gone.

july.agricultural: Corn growing. Soybean cultivation. Second hay cutting. Lay-by equivalent — less labor-intensive than the hand-cultivation era.
july.social_civic: Fourth of July — parades, barbecues, fireworks. County fairs beginning. Revival season.
july.weather_conditions: Hottest month — 72-86F. Drought risk. Corn pollination period — drought during pollination was devastating.
july.narrative_hook: The Fourth of July in {town} was the one day the whole county showed up — {ancestor_name} at the parade, the barbecue, the fireworks, and the corn growing itself for the only week of the year that allowed a holiday.

august.agricultural: Corn filling. Soybeans maturing. Critical period — drought or excessive rain could devastate either crop.
august.social_civic: State Fair in Springfield (since 1853) — the premier agricultural and social event in the cluster. Revival season. County fairs.
august.narrative_hook: The Illinois State Fair in August was when {ancestor_name} brought the best ear of corn, the biggest hog, and the family to Springfield — where the livestock judging, the carnival, and the political speeches happened in the same week.

september.agricultural: Soybean harvest beginning (combines). Early corn harvest in some years. Hay final cutting.
september.social_civic: School opened. Rural schools still on agricultural calendars in some districts. Homecoming events.
september.economic: First harvest income from soybeans. Corn prices being watched.
september.narrative_hook: September was when the combine rolled into the soybean field and {ancestor_name} started counting — bushels per acre, price per bushel, and the arithmetic that would tell the year's story.

october.agricultural: Corn harvest — mechanical corn pickers by the 1930s, combines by the 1950s. The machine replaced the husking crew. Grain hauled to elevators or stored in cribs.
october.social_civic: Homecoming. Halloween. The last outdoor social events before winter.
october.economic: Primary income month. Grain sold at elevator or stored. The year's financial verdict.
october.weather_conditions: Cool — 50-60F. First frost by mid-October. Clear skies. The one comfortable month.
october.narrative_hook: October was harvest — {ancestor_name} running the picker through corn rows that stretched to the horizon, the machine doing in hours what the husking crew had done in days, and the price at the elevator determining whether the year's investment paid off.

november.agricultural: Late harvest. Fall plowing and tillage. Livestock consolidated.
november.social_civic: Thanksgiving. Hunting season. High school football championships.
november.economic: Final grain marketing. Year-end financial decisions.
november.weather_conditions: Cold arriving — 36-46F. Gray. First snow possible.
november.narrative_hook: Thanksgiving in {county} County was when {ancestor_name} could stop — the corn in the crib or the elevator, the fields turned for spring, and a few days before winter closed the prairie down.

december.agricultural: Livestock chores. Equipment winterization. Planning for next year's crop.
december.social_civic: Christmas. Church events. Community gatherings. Year-end.
december.economic: Year-end accounting. Tax planning. Seed and input orders for spring.
december.narrative_hook: Christmas in {county} County was coal-fire warm inside and prairie-cold outside — {ancestor_name} at the table with the family, the year's work measured and settled, and the corn crib visible through the kitchen window.

narrative_hooks: The corn calendar governed {county} County — planting in May, cultivating in June, harvesting in October — and {ancestor_name} lived by it as surely as the corn did.

The Illinois State Fair in August was when Springfield became the center of the agricultural world — {ancestor_name} brought the best of the farm and spent a week at the fairgrounds where the corn judging, the livestock ring, and the carnival all happened under the same prairie sun.

The Fourth of July was the one day the corn calendar allowed a holiday — {ancestor_name} at the parade in {town}, the barbecue, and the fireworks, while the corn grew itself for the only week of the year that did not demand labor.
October was the reckoning — the combine through the corn, the bushels weighed, the price posted, and {ancestor_name}'s year reduced to a number at the elevator.

Hog killing in December was the family's protein insurance — {ancestor_name} in the cold air, scalding the hog, rendering the lard, curing the hams, and saving every part because waste was something a corn farmer could not afford.

The tractor changed the scale but not the gamble — {ancestor_name} planted more corn in a day than {pronoun_possessive} grandfather had planted in a week, but the weather and the price were the same bet.
source_references: Bogue, Allan G. From Prairie to Corn Belt: Farming on the Illinois and Iowa Prairies in the Nineteenth Century. Chicago: University of Chicago Press, 1963.
Cronon, William. Nature's Metropolis: Chicago and the Great West. New York: W. W. Norton, 1991.
Power, John Carroll. History of the Early Settlers of Sangamon County, Illinois. Springfield, 1876.
Fraker, Guy C. Lincoln's Ladder to the Presidency. Southern Illinois University Press, 2012.
USDA Agricultural Census reports, various years.
